title-group.article-title: Anxiety in post-covid-19 syndrome – prevalence, mechanisms and treatment

contrib-group.aff: aLaboratory of Behavioral Medicine, Neuroscience Institute, Lithuanian University of Health Sciences, Vyduno al. 4, 00135 Palanga, Lithuania
bCenter for Behavioral Sciences and Mental Health, Istituto Superiore di Sanità, Viale Regina Elena, 299, 00161 Roma, Italy
cAlbert Einstein College of Medicine, Montefiore Medical Center, 3307 Bainbridge Avenue Bronx, NY 10467, USA
dIcahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, 1 Gustave L. Levy Pl., New York, NY 10029, USA
eIstituto di Neuroscienze Florence, Via A. Lamarmora 24, Firenze 50121, Italy
fDepartment of Psychiatry and Psychotherapy, Medical Center – University of Freiburg, Faculty of Medicine, University of Freiburg, Hauptstrasse 5, D-79104 Freiburg, Germany

author-notes.corresp: ∗Corresponding author. Department of Psychiatry and Psychotherapy, University of Freiburg, Hauptstrasse 5, Freiburg, D-79104, Germany. katharina.domschke@uniklinik-freiburg.de

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: In addition to the general impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on anxiety-related mental state, anxiety and anxiety disorders have also been implicated within ‘post-COVID-19 syndrome’, i.e. following a SARS-CoV-2 infection. The present narrative review provides an update on the current state of knowledge on anxiety and anxiety disorders in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome, on epidemiological, psychological, and biological factors that may contribute to anxiety following a SARS-CoV-2 infection, as well as on therapy options available for anxiety in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome. A multi-step systematic literature search of PubMed and Web of Science databases was performed applying the following broad search terms: (“anxiety” OR “anxiety disorder”) AND (“COVID” OR “COVID-19” OR “corona” OR “pandemic” OR “Post-COVID” OR “Long-COVID”). Eligible articles published until November 15, 2022 were included. Meta-analyses identified anxiety prevalence rates ranging from 16.6% to 29.6% after a SARS-CoV-2 infection. Premorbid anxiety has not reliably been shown to be associated with post-COVID-19 syndrome. Female sex, older age, severity of COVID-19 infection, hospitalization, reduced mobility, uncertainty, loneliness and low social support, anxiety sensitivity and cognitive inflexibility as well as biological factors such as immune dysregulation, alterations in the angiotensin system and hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal axis activation have been identified as potential anxiety risk factors in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome. Treatment options comprise pharmacotherapy with selective serotonin/noradrenaline reuptake inhibitors and (internet-/computer-based) cognitive behavioral psychotherapy, possibly augmented by mindfulness-based techniques, physical exercise and non-invasive brain stimulation. Provided corroboration of the currently suggested increased risk of anxiety in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome by future large-scale, prospective studies, a standardized screening for anxiety in patients with a SARS-CoV-2 infection – particularly in individuals with risk factors – and a psychiatric consultation and liaison service should be established to provide preventive and therapeutic interventions as early as possible.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Anxiety disorders – i.e. panic disorder (PD), generalized anxiety disorder (GAD), social anxiety disorder (SAD), agoraphobia, specific phobias, separation anxiety disorder and selective mutism (American Psychiatric et al., 2013) – are the most frequent mental disorders with a 12-month prevalence of 10–14% (Kessler et al., 2012; Wittchen et al., 2011) and confer a considerable socioeconomic disease burden (Gustavsson et al., 2011; Penninx et al., 2021). According to the Global Burden of Diseases, Injuries and Risk Factors Study, in 2019 anxiety disorders ranked seventh among all diseases worldwide in 15–24 year olds and fifteenth among 25–49 year olds in terms of ‘Disability-Adjusted Life-Years' (DALYs) (Collaborators, 2022). Additionally, anxiety disorders carry a significantly increased risk of sequential comorbidity with depressive episodes (Meier et al., 2015), alcohol use disorders (Grant et al., 2016) and chronic somatic disorders, particularly in females (Bobo et al., 2022).

In the context of the corona virus (SARS-CoV-2; COVID-19) pandemic that emerged in December 2019, the prevalence and burden of anxiety-related symptoms seem to have increased even more. In a study conducted with approximately 336,500 individuals in the general US population in April/May 2020, more than 30% of participants scored above the cut score value of the two-item Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD-2) scale, which represented a threefold increase compared with corresponding values dating from 2019 (Twenge and Joiner, 2020). In Germany, 17.1% of 6509 respondents in March/April 2020 reported that their COVID-19-related anxiety was exaggerated, with 25.1% stating that they experienced limitations in daily life due to this anxiety (Petzold et al., 2020). Additionally, data from the German National Cohort (NAKO) in a sample of 113,928 respondents interviewed in April/May 2020 showed that mainly female gender and young age were risk factors for increased anxiety (seven-item GAD scale, GAD-7) in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic (Peters et al., 2020). Accordingly, a meta-analysis of 43 international studies published between January 2019 and May 2020 with a total of 71,613 participants regarding various dimensional anxiety measures (e.g., GAD-2, GAD-7, State-Trait Anxiety Questionnaire [STAI], Self-Rating Anxiety Scale [SAS], Depression, Anxiety and Stress Scale [DASS-21 anxiety], Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale [HADS anxiety], Symptom Checklist [SCL-90 anxiety]) discerned an incipient increase in anxiety symptoms (standardized mean difference [SMD] = 0.40; 95% CI = 0.15–0.65) in the context of the early phases of the pandemic (Kunzler et al., 2021). A more recent meta-analysis of data collected in 204 countries from January 2020 to January 2021 identified subclinical anxiety to have increased by 25.6% compared with pre-pandemic times (Santomauro et al., 2021). Risk factors identified for an exacerbation of already existing anxiety or the first occurrence of anxiety symptoms in the pandemic were: female gender, young age, increased media consumption, affiliation with health professions, pre-existing mental or physical illnesses, financial problems and job insecurity. On a socioeconomic level, in 2020 subclinical anxiety was responsible for an additional 9.05 million DALYs worldwide, primarily among women and those of younger age (Santomauro et al., 2021). The overall global prevalence of anxiety during the COVID-19 pandemic until June 2021 was estimated to be as high as 35.1% in a meta-analysis of 194 studies comprising >2.4 million participants (Delpino et al., 2022).

In addition to the general impact of the pandemic on anxiety-related mental state briefly reviewed above, anxiety and anxiety disorders have also been implicated within ‘post-COVID-19 syndrome’, i.e. following a SARS-CoV-2 infection (Nalbandian et al., 2021; Penninx et al., 2022). The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence defined different stages of COVID-19 disease based on emergence or persistence of COVID-19-associated symptoms in relation to the time of initial manifestation of this disease (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, 2020): the ‘acute COVID-19’ disease encompasses the first 4 weeks, whereas ‘ongoing symptomatic COVID-19’ disease is defined to occur 5–12 weeks after initial manifestation; and symptoms that persist for longer than 12 weeks and are not assignable to an alternative diagnosis are subsumed under the term ‘post-COVID-19 syndrome’. The World Health Organization defined post-COVID-19 syndrome as a condition that is present in persons with a history of probable or confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection 3 months after the onset of COVID-19 symptoms, that persists for at least 2 months and that is not explainable by an alternative diagnosis (Soriano et al., 2022). Symptoms most commonly include fatigue, dyspnea, and cognitive dysfunction, affecting daily functioning levels, as well as anxiety and depression (Carod-Artal, 2021; Hellwig and Domschke, 2022; Mazza et al., 2022; Premraj et al., 2022). These symptoms persist with the onset of infection or manifest only after recovery from acute illness. They may fluctuate or recur during the course.

While a plethora of articles have highlighted the impact of the pandemic on anxiety symptoms in general as mentioned above (Delpino et al., 2022), the present narrative review – updating and extending two previous review articles dedicated to that particular topic based on the state of knowledge in October 2020 (Uzunova et al., 2021) and July 2022 (Efstathiou et al., 2022), respectively – will focus on anxiety and anxiety disorders in the context of an actual infection with SARS-CoV-2, either as a sequela within the framework of post-COVID-19 syndrome (Section 3.1) or as a predisposing factor for developing post-COVID-19 syndrome (Section 3.2). This review will also present current epidemiological, psychological and biological theories on the pathogenesis of anxiety in the context of a SARS-CoV-2 infection (Section 3.3) as well as treatment options on several levels (Section 3.4).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A multi-step systematic literature search of PubMed and Web of Science databases was performed by independent researchers J.B. and K.D. applying the following broad search terms: (“anxiety” OR “anxiety disorder”) AND (“COVID” OR “COVID-19” OR “corona” OR “pandemic” OR “Post-COVID” OR “Long-COVID”). Identified articles published until November 15, 2022 were screened based on title and abstract reading. Only peer-reviewed studies published in English reporting on original research or review articles were considered eligible to be included in the present review. After exclusion of articles irrelevant for the topic investigated, full texts of the remaining articles were further assessed for inclusion. Searching reference lists of selected articles and pertinent review articles revealed additional eligible articles. Disagreements in search and selection were resolved through discussion and consensus.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Paragraph 3.1 and Table 1 report on the available meta-analytic studies and systematic reviews, paragraph 3.2, and supplementary table S1 display the characteristics of the original research studies on the topic (n = 40). Most of the original studies included in the present review were published in 2022 (Thompson et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2022; Taquet et al., 2022; Kucukkarapinar et al., 2022; Battistella et al., 2022; Magnúsdóttir et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Cattarinussi et al., 2022; Homann et al., 2022; Hazumi et al., 2022; Fiabane et al., 2022; Evans et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2022; Azevedo et al., 2022; Azizi et al., 2022; Houben-Wilke et al., 2022; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2022; Woodward et al., 2022) (n = 17), followed by studies published in 2021 (Al-Aly et al., 2021; Taquet et al., 2021a; Taquet et al., 2021b; Menges et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021a; Huang et al., 2021b; Abel et al., 2021; Taquet et al., 2021c; Klaser et al., 2021; Parker et al., 2021; Hellgren et al., 2021; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2021; Frontera et al., 2021; Todt et al., 2021; Nie et al., 2021) (n = 15) and studies in 2020 (Iqbal et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020a; Tomasoni et al., 2021; Dai et al., 2020; Mazza et al., 2020a) (n = 8).Table 1
Meta-analytic studies or systematic reviews investigating anxiety in individuals with COVID-19 infection.Table 1
No.Study (authors/year/design)Patient sample (N, f, m, age [SD])COVID-19 infection specificationTime frame of anxiety evaluationAnxiety prevalence/rating scales/cut scoresRemarks
1.Dong et al., 2021 (Dong et al., 2021)/Systematic review and meta-analysisN = 8587 (mean age, sex not provided)Individuals who experienced COVID-19Not providedGeneral prevalence of any anxiety symptoms: 39.6% (30.8–48.5%), 27 studies, 5144 cases).Moderate anxiety: 16.6% (10.1–23.1%).
SAS, n = 8 studies; GAD-7, n = 7 studies; HADS, n = 4 studies,For patients who received a follow-up investigation (one week to three months post-discharge), combined prevalence of anxiety was 44.9% (33.6–56.3%)
SCL-90, n = 4 studies; HAMA, n = 2 studies; GAD-2, n = 2 studies
2.Groff et al., 2021 (Groff et al., 2021)/Systematic review and meta-analysisN = 250,351 (mean age: 54 [SD]: 9 years, m = 56%, 79% hospitalized due to COVID-19)Survivors of COVID-191 month (short-term): 13 studies; 2–5 months (intermediate-term): 38 studies;6 months (long-term): 8 studiesMedian (IQR), 29.6% (14.0–44.0%), 7 studies, 600 cases–
GAD-7, n = 6 studies; HADS, n = 1 study
No cut score given
3.Premraj et al., 2022 (Premraj et al., 2022)/Systematic review and meta-analysisN = 10,530 (f = 59%; mean age: 52 years [SD: 10]Post-COVID-19 syndrome (symptoms that developed or persisted ≥3 months after the onset of COVID-19)≥3 months post onset of COVID-19;23% (14–32%), 8 studies, number of cases not specifiedAnxiety was more frequently reported in the community than in patients hospitalized during the acute phase of COVID-19 (31% vs.16%)
More than half of all patients were hospitalized during their acute-phase COVID-19 infection (51%) and 13% were admitted to an ICU)mid-term (3–6 months);Questionnaires not specifiedAnxiety was substantially increased in prevalence long-term versus mid-term
long-term (>6 months); follow-up after acute COVID-19
4.Badenoch et al., 2022 (Badenoch et al., 2022)/Systematic review and meta-analysisN = 18,917 (mean age: 51 years [SD: 9], 31% did not specify sex, m = 33%,Individuals who had experienced COVID-19<12 weeks in 10 studies;General prevalence of anxiety: 19.1% (13.3–26.8%), 14 studies 3716 cases<12 weeks follow-up, n = 10 studies, 2537 cases: anxiety prevalence = 17.0% (11.6–24.2%)
57% hospitalized due to COVID-19)≥12 weeks in 2 studiesGAD-7, n = 4 studies;≥12 weeks follow-up, n = 2 studies, 672 cases: anxiety prevalence = 20.0% (4.0–60.1%)
Mean follow-up of 77 days (range: 14–182 days)HADS, n = 2 studies;
STAI-state, n = 1 study;
self-rating anxiety scale, n = 1 study
Not specified, n = 8 studies
Cut score given only for 1 study (GAD-7 > 9)
5.Yang et al., 2022 (Yang et al., 2022)/Systematic review and meta-analysisN = 88,769 (mean age, sex not provided)Individuals previously hospitalized with COVID-193–4 monthsAnxiety measures not reported.Anxiety prevalence is reported to be higher in Europe than in Asia
5–8 months20.9% (13.4–31.2%), 11 studies, number of cases not specified
≥9 months17.9% (11.3–27.2%), 8 studies, number of cases not specified
12.9% (7.7–29.0%), 5 studies, number of cases not specified
Legend to Table 1: Coronavirus disease, COVID-19; Female, f; Two-item Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale, GAD-2; Hamilton Anxiety Scale, HAMA; Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale, HADS; Male, m; Self-Rating Anxiety Scale, SAS; Seven-item GAD scale, GAD-7; Symptom checklist-90, SCL-90; State-Trait Anxiety Questionnaire, STAI.

Countries reporting post-COVID-19 anxiety data included Brazil (Battistella et al., 2022; Azevedo et al., 2022; Todt et al., 2021), China (Xiao et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2021a, 2021b; Nie et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020a; Dai et al., 2020), Germany (Homann et al., 2022), Italy (Cattarinussi et al., 2022; Fiabane et al., 2022; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2022; Tomasoni et al., 2021; Mazza et al., 2020a), Japan (Hazumi et al., 2022), Morocco (Azizi et al., 2022), Qatar (Iqbal et al., 2020), Spain (Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2021), Switzerland, (Menges et al., 2021), Sweden (Hellgren et al., 2021), Turkey (Kucukkarapinar et al., 2022), the UK (Thompson et al., 2022; Evans et al., 2022; Abel et al., 2021), and the USA (Xie et al., 2022; Taquet et al., 2021a, 2021b, 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Woodward et al., 2022; Al-Aly et al., 2021; Klaser et al., 2021; Parker et al., 2021; Frontera et al., 2021). Three studies used multi-country data in their analysis, with one including Denmark, Estonia, Iceland, Norway, Sweden and the UK (Magnúsdóttir et al., 2022), the second including the USA, Australia, the UK, Spain, Bulgaria, India, Malaysia and Taiwan (Taquet et al., 2022), and the third including the Netherlands and Belgium (Houben-Wilke et al., 2022).

Most of the included studies employed a prospective cohort design (Xie et al., 2022; Battistella et al., 2022; Magnúsdóttir et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Evans et al., 2022; Azizi et al., 2022; Houben-Wilke et al., 2022; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2022; Al-Aly et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021a; Huang et al., 2021b; Abel et al., 2021; Parker et al., 2021; Hellgren et al., 2021; Frontera et al., 2021; Todt et al., 2021), followed by cross-sectional studies (Cattarinussi et al., 2022; Homann et al., 2022; Hazumi et al., 2022; Azevedo et al., 2022; Nie et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020a; Tomasoni et al., 2021; Dai et al., 2020), retrospective cohort studies (Fiabane et al., 2022; Taquet et al., 2021a, 2021b, 2021c; Iqbal et al., 2020) and prospective case-control studies (Kucukkarapinar et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2022). One study analysed the data of eleven prospective cohort studies in the UK (Thompson et al., 2022).

The studies used different approaches to define the diagnosis of anxiety disorders or the severity of anxiety symptoms. Several studies relied on data registry entries or psychiatric interviews (Xie et al., 2022; Taquet et al., 2021a, 2021b, 2021c, 2022; Al-Aly et al., 2021; Abel et al., 2021; Iqbal et al., 2020). The most widely used questionnaire for assessing anxiety symptom/disorder severity was the GAD-7 (Thompson et al., 2022; Cattarinussi et al., 2022; Homann et al., 2022; Hazumi et al., 2022; Fiabane et al., 2022; Evans et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020), followed by the HADS (Thompson et al., 2022; Azevedo et al., 2022; Azizi et al., 2022; Houben-Wilke et al., 2022; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2022; Parker et al., 2021; Hellgren et al., 2021; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020a; Tomasoni et al., 2021). Other studies employed the GAD-2 (Wang et al., 2022; Klaser et al., 2021), the 5-level EQ-5D version (EQ-5D-5L) (Battistella et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2021a, 2021b), the SAS (Nie et al., 2021; Dai et al., 2020), the STAI (Woodward et al., 2022; Mazza et al., 2020a), the DASS-21 (Kucukkarapinar et al., 2022; Menges et al., 2021), the Emotional State Questionnaire (EST-Q2) (Magnúsdóttir et al., 2022), the Neuro-QoL (Frontera et al., 2021), and the four-item Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-4) (Todt et al., 2021).

sec.title: Anxiety/anxiety disorders in post-COVID-19 syndrome
sec.p: To date, five meta-analyses and systematic reviews have been published documenting an increased rate of anxiety symptoms in individuals after a SARS-CoV-2 infection (Premraj et al., 2022; Dong et al., 2021; Badenoch et al., 2022; Groff et al., 2021) (for details see Table 1). Before summarizing these findings, it is important to mention that a previous meta-analysis of three studies conducted in 2005 through 2009 comprising 3559 individuals after a SARS-CoV-1 infection identified anxiety symptoms in 14.8% (11.1–19.4%) of the patients (Rogers et al., 2020). Recent meta-analyses focusing on anxiety in the aftermath of a SARS-CoV-2 infection identified markedly increased anxiety prevalence rates, in most of the cases defined using various questionnaires cut scores, ranging from 16.6% (10.1–23.1%) to 29.6% (14.0–44.0%): One of the first meta-analyses in this regard by Dong et al. (2021) in 8587 individuals after a SARS-CoV-2 infection initially suggested that anxiety symptoms were prevalent in 39.6% (30.8–48.5%) of individuals; however, when adjusting their findings and including only those with moderate to severe anxiety, the prevalence rate was found to be much lower at 16.6% (10.1–23.1%). Unfortunately, a mean follow-up time was not reported by the study authors. In another meta-analysis published by Groff et al. (2021) in 250,351 COVID-19 survivors, GAD was identified in 29.6% of the individuals (14.0–44.0%) (Groff et al., 2021). A recent meta-analysis by Premraj et al. (2022) in 10,530 individuals with post-COVID-19 syndrome as defined by National Institute of Clinical Excellence guidelines, identified the anxiety prevalence rate to be 23% (14–32%), where anxiety symptoms were more frequently reported in community samples than in patients hospitalized during their acute phase of COVID-19 (31% vs. 16%). Additionally, anxiety was substantially increased in prevalence when comparing long-term (>6 months) versus mid-term (3–6 months) rates. In another recent meta-analysis published by Badenoch et al. (2022) in 18,917 individuals who experienced COVID-19, authors discerned anxiety symptoms to be prevalent in 19.1% (13.3–26.8%) of individuals (Badenoch et al., 2022). In the latest meta-analysis, Yang et al. (2022) collected data from 88,769 individuals previously hospitalized with COVID-19 (Yang et al., 2022). Three follow-up periods were identified, with anxiety rates of 20.9% (13.4–31.2%) after 3–4 months, 17.9% (11.3–27.2%) after 5–8 months, and 12.9% (7.7–29.0%) after ≥9 months. Authors noted that the identified anxiety prevalence rate was considerably higher in Europe than in Asia.

The reported meta-analyses did not focus explicitly on anxiety symptoms, but included several other neuropsychiatric symptoms as well. As not all studies provide a precise follow-up duration, the number of hospitalized cases, or other relevant information such as comorbidities, a synoptic interpretation of the currently available data is not possible.

Several recent large-scale studies providing original data offer further insight into anxiety prevalence rates in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome (for details see supplementary table S1). These studies mainly suggest anxiety to be increased in individuals post COVID-19 in comparison to those who have not been infected. The risk seems to remain elevated for a period of up to two months. For example, in a cohort follow-up study by Abel et al. (2021) conducted in the UK (N = 11, 923, 105) individuals who tested positive for COVID-19 were followed up for ten months. The authors found a formal anxiety incidence rate of 20.1% per 1000 person-years in individuals with COVID-19, whereas it was 11.2% per 1000 person-years for unexposed individuals. Accordingly, anxiety prevalence rate was higher in individuals with COVID-19 in comparison to individuals without COVID-19 (13.4% vs. 10.9%). A similar large-scale (N = 5,064,270) study conducted in the USA by Al-Aly et al. (2021) found that for individuals with COVID-19 the incidence rate of anxiety disorders (ICD-10 criteria) per 1000 person-years at 6-month follow-up was 31.6%. Xie et al. (2022) later re-analysed the same database used by Al-Aly et al. (2021), investigating the risk of anxiety disorders in individuals with confirmed COVID-19 disease within a period of 30 days to approximately 1 year after infection. Having COVID-19 was associated with a significantly increased risk of also developing an anxiety disorder and this was especially true for those individuals who had been hospitalized due to their COVID-19 disease (Xie et al., 2022). Taquet et al. (2022) compared individuals with COVID-19 to a cohort with other respiratory illnesses. Authors discovered that those with COVID-19 had a considerably greater incidence of anxiety disorders compared to individuals with other respiratory diseases, especially six months after infection. A ′risk horizon’ analysis revealed a return to baseline for the control group after 58 days: that is, if no anxiety disorder had occurred after 58 days in individuals primarily diagnosed with COVID-19, the risk for anxiety disorder after a COVID-19 diagnosis was no higher than for other respiratory disease. The incidence of anxiety disorders in COVID-19 patients was equivalent to that of the matched control group after 417 days. The aforementioned studies provide some insight into the incidence, prevalence and risk ratio of anxiety (disorders) after COVID-19. The possibility of bias in the data is decreased when large sample sizes and a standardized method of data collecting are used. Incomplete data, registry codes that do not adequately represent the real diagnosis of the patient, the fact that only those individuals who sought medical assistance were included as well as self-rating questionnaires employed to define anxiety constitute potential biases and limit the generalizability of the available results.

Further studies employed the GAD-2 scale to measure anxiety in the context of a COVID-19 infection: Exemplarily, in a community-based survey study by Klaser et al. (2021) 413,148 individuals with self-reported COVID-19 were followed up in a time period of 1–4 months after diagnosis. Anxiety and depression, as measured with the GAD-2 scale and the PHQ-2 (with a cut score ≥3 indicating probable anxiety or depression), were slightly more prevalent in previously SARS-CoV-2- positive (30.4%) vs SARS-CoV-2-negative (26.1%) individuals. Similarly, in a cohort study (N = 54,960) conducted in the USA and Canada by Wang et al. (2022) the COVID-19 group displayed an anxiety prevalence (GAD-2 scale with a cut score ≥3 indicating probable anxiety) of 20.4%. Symptoms of depression, anxiety, worry and perceived stress at baseline were furthermore found to be associated with a 25–51% increased risk of having symptoms that interfered with activities, from ‘occasionally’ to ‘always’.

These studies used very a short forms of anxiety assessment (GAD-2), consisting of only two and quite unspecific questions to measure the severity of symptoms. This type of assessments is more effective in large sample studies and may increase the response rate, because the completion requires less time and effort from participants, providing a quick snapshot of anxiety symptoms. However, utilizing a two-question rating scale has the disadvantage of not being able to capture the wider symptomatology of anxiety disorders. This is particularly true when using a follow-up design, as it is possible that this form of questioning lacks the sensitivity to identify minor changes in anxiety symptoms over time.

However, similar anxiety prevalence characteristics were obtained in a study by Thompson et al. analysing results from 11 UK longitudinal population studies employing both the GAD-2 and longer versions such as the GAD-7 and the HADS (Thompson et al., 2022). Here, a self-reported COVID-19 infection was longitudinally associated with a deterioration in mental health and life satisfaction; however, participants who had a positive serology as identified by polymerase chain reaction, but did not self-report a COVID-19 infection, did not show an association with mental health outcomes.

The aforementioned findings were later confirmed by a study employing summary statistics from large Genome-Wide Association Studies on COVID-19 susceptibility and using a Mendelian randomization approach to investigate whether COVID-19 increases the risk of neuropsychiatric sequelae (Tirozzi et al., 2022). This study suggested that COVID-19 is associated with subsequent anxiety and that those previously hospitalized due to infection are particularly vulnerable.

Besides the studies showing overall trend of higher anxiety levels in individuals with post-COVID-19 syndrome in comparison to individuals who had not experienced COVID-19, several other studies suggested additional unfavourable outcomes associated with post-COVID-19 anxiety. An investigation by Mazza et al. (2020a) found that among COVID-19 survivors 55% of the patients displayed a pathological score for at least one disorder, including anxiety, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), depression, insomnia, and obsessive-compulsive disorders.

While the majority of these studies focused on adults, some also investigated post-COVID-19 symptoms in children. A large-scale retrospective cohort study by Kostev et al. (2022) included children and adolescents who were diagnosed with COVID-19 (N = 6,568, f = 3231 [49.2%]; mean [SD] age = 10.1 [4.9] years). Anxiety prevalence was higher in children with post-COVID-19 syndrome (5.3%) in comparison to children without post-COVID-19 syndrome (1.2%). According to the most recent review summarizing 48 studies of post-COVID-19 symptoms in children, long COVID symptoms in children are related to specific mental and physical deficits that have the potential to influence long-term functioning and raise the total burden on health care delivery (Gupta et al., 2022).

sec.title: Anxiety/anxiety disorders predisposing towards post-COVID-19 syndrome
sec.p: Besides several studies suggesting anxiety symptoms or anxiety disorders to arise as part of post-COVID-19 syndrome, there is a controversial debate ongoing whether pre-existing anxiety might predispose toward post-COVID-19 syndrome or neuropsychiatric symptoms and anxiety in particular in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome.

One study completed in the USA and Canada (N = 54,960; 96.6% female) reported probable premorbid anxiety measured via the GAD-2 (cut score ≥3) and worry about COVID-19 to be associated with post-COVID-19 syndrome (Wang et al., 2022). The results of this study suggest that a mental stress constellation that existed before a SARS-CoV-2 infection may be a risk factor for post-COVID-19 syndrome. A Japanese study in 6016 self-identified COVID-19 individuals showed that the severity of anxiety measured with the GAD-7 (cut score ≥10) increased progressively in COVID-19-recovered individuals with a psychiatric history (6-month follow-up), but not in those without a psychiatric history. A study by Zhang et al. (2020a) in hospitalized individuals (N = 296) diagnosed with mild symptoms of COVID-19 showed that individuals with lower levels of resilience experienced more anxiety.

In contrast, Abel et al. investigating >10 million individuals found that in individuals with pre-existing common mental illness (i.e., depression or anxiety disorders) there was no association of a positive SARS-CoV-2 test result with an increased risk of subsequent depression or anxiety events (Abel et al., 2021). These findings were further confirmed by Homann et al. (2022) who observed that, among other psychological characteristics such as fatigue, anxiety in individuals with post-COVID-19 was not associated with pre-existing anxiety levels. Also, in several studies investigating COVID-19-related mortality, in contrast to the presence of any mental disorder, anxiety was not associated with an increased risk of COVID-19 mortality (Nemani et al., 2021; Vai et al., 2021; Fond et al., 2021).

sec.title: Mechanisms of anxiety/anxiety disorders risk in post-COVID-19 syndrome
sec.p:
Epidemiological, psychological and biological risk factors potentially predisposing to or mediating anxiety or anxiety disorders in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome are reported below and depicted in Fig. 1.Fig. 1
Epidemiological, psychological and biological risk factors of anxiety in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome Legend to Fig. 1: ACE: angiotensin-converting enzyme; HPA: hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal axis; IL: interleukin; OFC: orbitofrontal cortex; PTSD: post-traumatic stress disorder.Fig. 1

sec.title: Epidemiological risk factors
sec.p: Several epidemiological risk factors have been suggested for anxiety in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome, including older age, severity of COVID-19 infection, hospitalization and a longer hospital stay (Thompson et al., 2022; Taquet et al., 2022; Kucukkarapinar et al., 2022; Battistella et al., 2022; Azizi et al., 2022; Menges et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021a; Todt et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020). For example, a large-scale multi-country study by Magnusdottir et al. (Magnúsdóttir et al., 2022) (N = 247,249) showed that individuals diagnosed with COVID-19 but who were never bedridden due to their illness were consistently at a lower risk of anxiety than those not diagnosed with COVID-19, whereas individuals who were bedridden for more than 7 days were persistently at higher risk of anxiety than those not diagnosed throughout the study period. Older adults might be at a higher risk of developing post-COVID-19 anxiety symptoms than younger individuals due to age-related factors such as cognitive complications (Hampshire et al., 2021), other somatic health problems and physical distancing imposed by COVID-19 restrictions (Robb et al., 2020). Furthermore, the severity of COVID-19 infection may cause worry and anxiety in those who are infected, particularly if they need hospitalization or intensive care, have reduced mobility or physical activity (Santomauro et al., 2021; Cerasola et al., 2022). Female gender was also found to be associated with hospitalization due to COVID-19, along with increased post-illness anxiety and somatic symptom levels (Evans et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2022; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2022; Taquet et al., 2021c; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2021; Nie et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020a). For instance, in the two prospective studies by Fernández-de-las-Peñas et al. (Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2022; Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2021) including individuals hospitalized due to COVID-19 (N = 1969) female gender, number of symptoms at hospital admission and days in hospital were associated with anxiety symptoms at 8-months follow-up.

sec.title: Psychological risk factors
sec.p: Having a family member or a colleague with COVID-19 constitutes a psychological risk factor for anxiety in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome (Zhang et al., 2020a; Dai et al., 2020; Maresca et al., 2022). Indeed anxiety might be associated with worries concerning individuals’ own health and the health of family members (Cheng et al., 2004). The number of physical symptoms and their change after hospitalization might also represent a risk towards developing post-COVID-19 anxiety (Dai et al., 2020). Both factors link anxiety to uncertainty about illness progression (Levine et al., 2022). Furthermore, probable anxiety caused by COVID-19 concern might evolve into post-COVID-19 anxiety because it entails chronic dread and worry about the condition (Wang et al., 2022; Klaser et al., 2021), which can be overwhelming and difficult to control underlining that uncertainty attitudes towards illness might further increase anxiety (Millroth and Frey, 2021).

Loneliness and being without a partner (Wang et al., 2022; Klaser et al., 2021) also may contribute to post-COVID-19 anxiety by increasing social isolation (Okruszek et al., 2020; Palgi et al., 2020) and decreasing social support (Dong et al., 2021; Szkody et al., 2021).

A recent study suggested that post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) symptoms were disproportionately higher in individuals needing in-patient admission, particularly those requiring breathing assistance, compared to those with minor COVID-19 symptoms handled at home (Chamberlain et al., 2021). Experiencing PTSD symptoms was considered a risk factor for post-COVID-19 anxiety (Houben-Wilke et al., 2022).

In a recent review by Zvolensky et al. (2022), interoceptive fear and anxiety-related processes (e.g., anxiety sensitivity, health anxiety and COVID-19 anxiety, fear and worry) were suggested to be linked with post-COVID-19 mental health conditions. The authors proposed that anxiety sensitivity, i.e. the inclination to interpret bodily symptoms as threatening, might enhance emotional discomfort associated with viral exposure or pandemic stress. This notion is further endorsed by other authors, suggesting that individuals with panic disorder for example are more prone to struggle with a condition such as COVID-19 because it may produce a variety of respiratory symptoms, which resemble those occurring during a panic attack (Perna and Caldirola, 2020).

sec.title: Biological risk factors
sec.p: The biological link between COVID-19 and anxiety is complex and still poorly understood (Penninx et al., 2022; Uzunova et al., 2021; Zawilska and Kuczynska, 2022). While some studies reported that COVID-19 does not enter the central nervous system and thus does not produce any direct action (Matschke et al., 2020; Cosentino et al., 2021; Thakur et al., 2021a), others found that the virus may reach the brain (Chen et al., 2022; Meinhardt et al., 2021). Overall, multiple mechanisms appear to be involved in mediating the impact of the virus on anxiety-related symptomatology.

Among these, overactivation of the immune system appears to be one of the most relevant mechanisms (Benedetti et al., 2021): In symptomatic patients, COVID-19 has been found associated with a marked immune dysregulation that includes cytokine release syndrome and dysregulated innate and adaptive responses (Shivshankar et al., 2022; Wong and Perlman, 2022). The interplay between the immune system and the brain has recently emerged as being profoundly implicated in mental disorders (Leboyer et al., 2016; Pariante, 2017). It has indeed become clear that inflammatory markers participate in key brain functions in physiological and pathological conditions (Branchi et al., 2021). For instance, immune dysregulation has been found to impair neural plasticity at the molecular, cellular and behavioral levels (Golia et al., 2019; Alboni et al., 2016). During acute COVID-19, an association between systemic immune activation and the severity of psychiatric symptoms has been observed (Benedetti et al., 2021). In particular, interleukin-6 (IL-6) levels in in-patient non-intensive care unit individuals with COVID-19 were linked with patients’ HADS-A score at baseline (Parker et al., 2021), and increased inflammatory markers at baseline have been reported to predict anxiety symptoms at follow-up (Mazza et al., 2020b). Similarly, IL-1 levels were found to be associated with anxiety (GAD-7) in a cross-sectional study investigating persons with severe COVID-19 admitted to the hospital (Hu et al., 2020). This connection is consistent with previous studies reporting an association between peripheral inflammation (e.g., increase in IL-1β, IL-6, IL-18, and tumor necrosis factor alpha [TNF-α] levels) and anxiety symptoms (Mazza et al., 2022). High levels of inflammatory markers have been reported not only at the peripheral but also at the central level. Neuroimaging studies reported high levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β, IL-6 and IL-8 in the cerebrospinal fluid of COVID-19 patients (Farhadian et al., 2020; Manganotti et al., 2021). However, it is not clear whether these markers are produced within the central nervous system or are first released in the periphery and then recruited into the brain. Indeed, systemic inflammation, including cytokine release, is associated with the production of chemoattractants by glial cells that recruit immune cells in the central nervous system (Francistiova et al., 2021). In addition, the impaired blood-brain barrier integrity reported in COVID-19 patients by brain magnetic resonance imaging studies (Lersy et al., 2021) may exacerbate the severe inflammatory response within the brain (Hernandez-Parra et al., 2023). The relevance of the activation of the immune system in the psychopathological sequelae triggered by severe COVID-19 is confirmed by the correlation between acute psychiatric symptoms and immune activation both at the systemic (Mazza et al., 2020b) and the brain levels (Moonis et al., 2021).

The brain structural and functional alterations associated with immune overactivation in COVID-19 patients have been proposed as a potential mechanism linking the infection to psychiatric symptoms such as anxiety (Benedetti et al., 2021). Indeed, structural changes in the central nervous system have been widely reported following the infection (Douaud et al., 2022; Tian et al., 2022; Newcombe et al., 2021). Neuroimaging studies comparing brain structure in recovered and control patients reported numerous alterations including a reduced thickness of grey matter in the orbitofrontal cortex and parahippocampal gyrus, markers of oedema and tissue damage in the region connected to the olfactory cortex and an overall reduced brain size (Douaud et al., 2022). In a large percentage of COVID-19 patients, alterations have been found also at the microstructural level and in the cerebral blood flow (Lu et al., 2020; Qin et al., 2021). Microstructure alterations in the grey and white matter and an impaired functional connectivity have been found associated with psychiatric symptoms in infected patients (Benedetti et al., 2021). It is worth noting that the structural alterations following the infection concern brain regions involved in psychiatric domains particularly relevant for anxiety, such as cognitive function and emotional regulation (Dresler et al., 2013), reported to be disrupted by COVID-19.

Multiple other potential biological mechanisms may link COVID-19 infection to anxiety disorders. For instance, altered angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE)/angiotensin II receptor type 2 function, which is part of the virus entry mechanism (Shang et al., 2020), might confer a higher vulnerability to anxiety as an insertion/deletion polymorphism in the ACE gene has been associated with panic disorder (Bandelow et al., 2010) and as angiotensin II type 2 receptor-expressing neurons in the central amygdala have been found to influence fear-related behaviour (Yu et al., 2019). Furthermore, the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, which is involved in many mental disorders including anxiety disorders (Zorn et al., 2017), has been reported to be dysregulated by infection with COVID-19 (Raony et al., 2020).

As a note of caution, the currently available studies on the link between COVID-19 and anxiety are still limited and report generally small effect sizes. In addition, it is not clear to what extent current findings can be specifically ascribed to COVID-19 or rather concern non-specific mechanisms such as hypoxia or inflammation (Penninx et al., 2022). Therefore, further studies are warranted.

sec.title: Treatment of anxiety/anxiety disorders in post-COVID-19 syndrome
sec.p: In the management of anxiety in the context of a COVID-19 infection, the following pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions are recommended depending on the condition of the patient as well as on the stage and severity of the COVID-19 infection (for an extensive review see (Uzunova et al., 2021)).

sec.title: Pharmacological treatment
sec.p: According to international guidelines (Bandelow et al., 2023), first-line anti-anxiety drugs, which primarily affect neuromodulators and neurotransmitters in the anxiety neurocircuitry, include selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs), tricyclic antidepressants (TCA) and – restricted to GAD – pregabalin. The SSRI fluvoxamine may be particularly effective in treating COVID-19-related anxiety, due to a suspected “cytokine storm suppressing” mechanism (Dobrodeeva et al., 2022). Furthermore, paroxetine was associated with a statistically significant decrease in risk of COVID-19 related mortality, and citalopram has been suggested as a candidate drug for being repurposed as a preventive treatment aimed at reducing COVID-19 patients' risk of progressing to severe stages of the disease (Visos-Varela et al., 2023). Pregabalin, which is a gabapentinoid that inhibits voltage-dependent calcium channels, is listed on Schedule V of the U.S.'s Controlled Substances Act and has been linked to cardiac side effects like heart failure and emphysema (Ho et al., 2013). In 2019, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) issued a warning about the risks of respiratory depression associated with using pregabalin and gabapentin, particularly when combined with opioids or in patients with respiratory issues (FDA, 2019). Thus, the use of gabapentinoids for managing anxiety in COVID-19 patients should be discouraged, especially in those with underlying cardiac/respiratory problems or more severe COVID-19. Non-selective benzodiazepines such as alprazolam, diazepam, clonazepam and lorazepam may cause respiratory adverse effects (Vozoris and Stephenson, 2015; Wang et al., 2018), and therefore should be avoided in COVID-19 patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and other respiratory problems.

In more experimental approaches, stress, anxiety and sadness have been suggested to be malleable by oxytocin (De Cagna et al., 2019). Oxytocin exerts immunostimulatory and anti-inflammatory effects, which may help to lessen the cytokine storm accompanying a COVID-19 infection (Uzunova et al., 2021; Imami et al., 2020). Intravenous oxytocin decreases TNF-α and IL-6 levels in humans and inhibits the proinflammatory cytokine response brought on by the bacterial endotoxin lipopolysaccharide (Clodi et al., 2008). As a result, oxytocin could be helpful as a complementary therapy for COVID-19 infection (Buemann et al., 2021; Thakur et al., 2021b), and it might also aid in stress and anxiety management at the same time (Uzunova G and Hollander, 2019).

The pineal hormone melatonin – exerting anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidative, neuroprotective and cardioprotective actions in addition to its ability to reduce anxiety (Repova et al., 2022) – may be especially helpful in preventing the cytokine storm in the context of a SARS-CoV-2 infection (Zhang et al., 2020b). In viral infections and pulmonary damage, melatonin has demonstrated therapeutic benefits. It has a highly favourable treatment profile, little adverse effects, and may be used safely by both adults and children.

Anti-cytokine therapies, such as monoclonal antibodies inhibiting IL-6 or IL-17, are likely to have therapeutic benefits against COVID-19-related anxiety given the significant role that pro-inflammatory cytokines are thought to play in the pathogenesis of COVID-19, ARDS, and anxiety (cf. 3.3.3.).

In conclusion, it is crucial to choose an anti-anxiety pharmacotherapy specifically for each individual patient, taking into account their condition, any underlying neuropsychiatric and general medical conditions, the drug's desired and unintended effects, any other medications they may be taking, any potential drug-drug interactions as well as – specifically in the context of a post-COVID-19 syndrome – the cortisol (Ramezani et al., 2020) and immune response (Hu et al., 2020).

sec.title: Psychotherapy
sec.p: According to international guidelines (Bandelow et al., 2023), cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is the first-line psychotherapeutic treatment option for anxiety disorders, which also seems to hold true in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome (Li et al., 2020). Given contact restrictions during the pandemic, interest in internet-/computer-based psychotherapy spiked (Hamlett et al., 2023). Indeed, different studies highlighted that internet-/computer-based psychotherapeutic interventions can decrease the levels of anxiety and increase resilience in patients with COVID-19 (Liu et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2020). Additionally, mindfulness-based as well as acceptance and commitment therapy interventions have shown promising results in COVID-19 infection related anxiety (Accoto et al., 2021; Håkansson et al., 2022).

sec.title: Other treatment options
sec.p: Non-pharmacological therapies such as non-invasive brain stimulation (NIBS) techniques, including transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) and photobiomodulation, could be beneficial in treating anxiety in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome. Transcranial DCS has been effectively used to treat anxiety in long-COVID syndrome (Shinjo et al., 2020). Protobiomodulation can target cytokines levels and reduce neuroinflammation, thus constituting a potential, yet still experimental treatment route for COVID-19 symptomatology (de Matos et al., 2021). A pilot study suggested significantly reduced severity of post-COVID anxiety after five neurofeedback sessions (Orendáčová et al., 2022). Exercise, particularly aerobic exercise, can help reduce anxiety (Henriksson et al., 2022), also in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome (Cerasola et al., 2022). Additionally, the immune system, the profile of pro-inflammatory cytokines, the heart and lungs, blood circulation, skeletal muscles, the brain, and the entire organism might benefit from moderate exercise (Codella et al., 2021).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Infection with SARS-CoV-2 has been linked to an increased risk of anxiety partly within the context of ‘post-COVID-19 syndrome’ in a variety of meta-analyses and original research articles. However, the available meta-analyses and original research works did not focus explicitly on anxiety symptoms. Also, results on the prevalence of anxiety symptoms differed between studies when post-COVID19 individuals were compared to negative controls or to survivors of other respiratory or infectious diseases. Finally, as not all studies provide data on an exact follow-up time, the number of hospitalized cases, and other important information such as somatic comorbidities, it is difficult to conclusively interpret the presently available evidence.

Given the high prevalence of anxiety symptoms in the context of post-COVID-19 syndrome as reviewed above, if corroborated by future, even larger scale prospective studies both in adults and children (Solmi et al., 2022a, 2022b), a comprehensive psychiatric consultation and liaison service in close cooperation with other disciplines should be established both on COVID-19 wards as well as with general practitioners in order to identify and treat persons at risk for developing anxiety symptoms or even anxiety disorders as early as possible thus reducing the additional high individual and socioeconomic disease burden typically associated with anxiety disorders (Hellwig and Domschke, 2022; Tang and Lee, 2022; Berlit and Domschke, 2022). It is recommended to use a standardized screening for anxiety in patients with a SARS-CoV-2 infection. For instance, the HADS has been suggested as a patient-reported outcome measure for assessing both anxiety and depressive symptoms with overall good psychometric properties in hospitalized COVID-19 survivors with post-COVID-19 syndrome (Fernández-de-Las-Peñas et al., 2022). However, it has to be kept in mind that a screening using relatively unspecific self-report scales such as the GAD-2 and GAD-7 containing questions such as “feeling nervous, anxious or on edge” or “not being able to stop or control worrying”, which measure dimensional symptoms rather than actual categorical diagnoses, and the short-lived nature of changes in mental health symptoms during a pandemic in general might have inflated the anxiety risk estimates in post-COVID-19 syndrome (Daly and Robinson, 2022; Taquet et al., 2021d). Additionally, subjective perception of the existence and severity of mental disorder symptoms might differ greatly across patients. For instance, it has been reported that post-COVID-19 patients with a severe acute illness complained little about their persistent deficits, whereas patients with a mild course scored significantly higher on the questionnaires for depression, anxiety and stress-associated symptoms. However, objectifiable cognitive deficits and a high prevalence of psychiatric symptoms were equally present in the subgroups (Voruz et al., 2022). Furthermore, results from a population-based French cohort study suggest that perception of persistent mental symptoms after COVID-19 infection was not associated with laboratory evidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection (Matta et al., 2022).

Particular attention needs to be paid to the apparently most vulnerable groups (see 3.3.), such as females and individuals living alone, with low social support, experiencing prior hospitalization or symptom worsening. Further “red-flag” risk factors calling for increased attention towards a possibly increased anxiety risk after SARS-CoV-2 infection comprise interoceptive anxiety-related processes such as increased anxiety sensitivity, psychological inflexibility, PTSD or obsessive compulsive and anankastic personality traits. Biological studies point towards immune system dysfunction, structural brain alterations, ACE/angiotensin II receptor 2 dysfunction or increased HPA-axis activity as potential mechanisms of an increased anxiety risk in the aftermath of a SARS-CoV-2 infection. Biological biomarkers indicating an increased risk of anxiety within post-COVID-19 syndrome prior to actual symptom manifestation, however, are still lacking (Abi-Dargham et al., 2023).

Treatment options of COVID-19 infection related anxiety comprise pharmacotherapy with selective serotonin/noradrenaline reuptake inhibitors and (internet-/computer-based) cognitive behavioral psychotherapy, possibly augmented by mindfulness-based techniques, physical exercise and non-invasive brain stimulation (see 3.4). Especially in the context of a pandemic requiring contact restrictions, internet-/computer-based therapies may provide a sustainable and flexible way to reduce increasing mental health issues. However, most studies regarding treatment options for COVID-19 infection related anxiety are still based on single cases, case series, or small sample sizes. High-quality and large clinical trials are needed to assess the efficacy of various treatment options in this context.

An analysis of psychological and biological risk factors in various other post-viral anxiety symptoms/syndromes – such as following an infection with influenza A (H1N1) and other influenza viruses, varicella-zoster virus, herpes simplex virus, human immunodeficiency virus/acquired immune deficiency syndrome, and hepatitis C – revealed a possible overlap with those found in the setting of post-COVID-19 syndrome (Coughlin, 2012; Tackey et al., 2023). Therefore, similar preventive as well as treatment methods might be applicable to anxiety occurring after infection with SARS-CoV-2 or any other severe viral infection (Li et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2020; Coughlin, 2012; Tackey et al., 2023).

In a preventive approach, it seems recommendable to make every effort to increase resilience towards anxiety during a pandemic as well as in the context of individual infections, by for instance strengthening social support from and connectedness with family and friends as well as by nuanced and balanced news coverage of pandemic-related contents (Manchia et al., 2022; Vinkers et al., 2020).

For future research it is important to emphasize that most studies reviewed here used screening questionnaires, rather than diagnostic tools, to assess possible anxiety symptoms. It is recommendable to use diagnostic tools that are tailored to specific anxiety disorders allowing for a true quantification of the potential future socioeconomic burden tied to anxiety disorders that might arise from post-COVID-19 syndrome.

In sum, in order to understand how a COVID-19 infection continues to influence mental health and anxiety risk particular in the longer term, longitudinal, well-controlled and sufficiently powered studies at (neuro)biological, clinical and societal levels are urgently warranted. As a lesson from this pandemic for the next, mental health should be prioritized in governmental and clinical strategies in an effort to protect those at risk, to rapidly identify and treat those affected, and to promote long-term resilience in the general population (Penninx et al., 2022).

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Conflict of interest
sec.p: JB works as a consultant at IQVIA. IB does not report any conflict of interest. SP has been or is a member of panels or has received speaker's honoraria in 2020: from Recordati, The Forensic Panel, L.E.K. Consulting, Biohaven Pharmaceuticals, Inc., EMBS Group spółka z ograniczoną odpowiedzialnością spółka komandytowa, Angelini Pharma, GEDEON RICHTER Plc, Association of Analytical Child and Adolescent Psychotherapists in Germany (Vereinigung Analytischer Kinder-u. Jugendlichen-Psychotherapeuten in Deutschland e. V., VAKJP), Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) and Innova Pharma; in 2021: from Recordati, Biohaven Pharmaceuticals, inc, Angelini Pharma, GW Research Limited, Innova Pharma, Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) for Spark funding instrument, Lundbeck, Otsuka Pharmaceutical, Aboca, Biogen and Menarini; in 2022: from Neopharmed, Gentili, Lundbeck, Biohaven Pharmaceuticals, Inc., Beckley Psytech, Medpace, Acsel Health and Biogen. KD has been a member of the Steering Committee Neurosciences and received speaker's honoraria from Janssen-Cilag GmbH.
